AI.motivation: Computing matrix pseudospectra over a prescribed region requires evaluating the smallest singular value at many grid points, which is prohibitively expensive for large-scale matrices.
AI.method: Exploits correlation between singular value problems at neighboring grid points by adaptively recycling singular subspaces from previously visited points via an iterative SVD solver, with fast Rayleigh-Ritz-SVD extraction and residual evaluation, plus two-level preconditioner recycling.
AI.result: Substantial speedups over existing methods while maintaining accuracy of computed pseudospectra.
AI.conclusion: Recycling singular subspaces is an effective acceleration strategy for large-scale pseudospectra computation for both dense and sparse matrices.

title: Almost Lipschitz regularity for solutions of elliptic equations with discontinuous coefficients
summary:
We are interested in the local higher integrability of solutions to elliptic equations with linear growth of the form
  $$-\text{ div}A(x,Du)=f(x). $$ Under a Besov regularity assumption both on the partial map $x \mapsto A(x,ξ)$ and the datum $f$, we prove that the solutions are almost Lipschitz continuous, i.e. their gradients belong locally to $L^q$, for any finite exponent $q$. In turn, solutions are locally $γ$-Hölder continuous, for every $γ\in (0,1)$. The difficulty arising from the lack of an explicit second variation for the problem is overcome by testing the equation with a function proportional to a power of the finite difference quotient of the solution. To the best of our knowledge, this technique is used in this context for the first time. We also provide an example showing the sharpness of our result in the scale of Lebesgue spaces.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that solutions to elliptic equations with linear growth are almost Lipschitz continuous (gradients in L^q for any finite q) under Besov regularity assumptions on the coefficient A(x,ξ) and datum f, using a novel technique based on finite difference quotients.
AI.motivation: To establish higher integrability and almost Lipschitz/Hölder regularity for solutions of elliptic equations under Besov-type assumptions, overcoming the difficulty of lacking explicit second variation.
AI.method: Testing the equation with a function proportional to a power of the finite difference quotient of the solution, a technique used for the first time in this context.
AI.result: Solutions have locally L^q gradients for any finite q (almost Lipschitz) and are γ-Hölder continuous for every γ∈(0,1). The result is sharp in the Lebesgue scale.
AI.conclusion: Besov regularity transfers to almost Lipschitz regularity for solutions; the method via finite difference quotients is effective where second variation methods fail.

title: Numerical analysis of first-order mean field games under displacement monotonicity
summary: We introduce a particle method for the numerical approximation of time-dependent first-order Mean Field Games (MFGs) systems with non-separable, displacement monotone Hamiltonians and terminal costs, for arbitrary time-horizons and (possibly) singular initial player distributions in $\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R}^d)$. The numerical scheme is based on an implicit Euler discretization in time and sampling in space of the characteristic Hamiltonian/Pontryagin system associated with the continuous MFGs system. We prove convergence of the approximations of the player distribution in the $L^{\infty}(\mathcal{W}_2)$-metric and the approximations for the gradient of the value function along optimal trajectories in the $L^{\infty}{(L^2)}$-norm as the number of spatial samples tends to infinity jointly with the temporal time-step vanishing. The error bound that we establish for this convergence further implies rates of convergence of the scheme for a range of spatial sampling techniques. Provided that the Lagrangian and terminal costs are additionally locally Lipschitz continuous, we also establish an asymptotic error bound in the $L^{\infty}(L^1)$-norm for the approximations of the value function along optimal trajectories. This is the first work in the literature on rigorous numerical approximation and analysis of first-order MFG systems that handles non-separable Hamiltonians and potentially singular initial agent distributions for arbitrary long time horizons. We illustrate the performance of the scheme in numerical experiments for a range of initial agent distributions, time horizons and space dimensions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a particle method for numerically approximating time-dependent first-order Mean Field Games (MFGs) with non-separable, displacement monotone Hamiltonians and terminal costs, achieving convergence guarantees for arbitrary time horizons and potentially singular initial player distributions.
AI.motivation: To develop and rigorously analyze a numerical scheme for first-order MFG systems that can handle non-separable Hamiltonians and singular initial distributions over arbitrary long time horizons, which had not been addressed in prior work.
AI.method: The scheme uses implicit Euler time discretization coupled with spatial sampling of the characteristic Hamiltonian/Pontryagin system associated with the continuous MFGs. Convergence is analyzed in $L^{\infty}(\mathcal{W}_2)$ for player distribution and $L^{\infty}(L^2)$ for value function gradients, with additional $L^{\infty}(L^1)$ bounds under Lipschitz continuity assumptions.
AI.result: Proves convergence rates for player distribution and value function gradient approximations as spatial samples increase and time steps vanish. Numerical experiments demonstrate performance across various initial distributions, time horizons, and space dimensions.
AI.conclusion: This is the first work providing rigorous numerical approximation and analysis for first-order MFGs with non-separable Hamiltonians and potentially singular initial distributions over arbitrary long time horizons.

title: Exact Blowup Analysis for the Weak-Advection Hou--Li Model
summary: We study self-similar singularity formation for the one-dimensional weak-advection Hou--Li model, a reduced model motivated by the axisymmetric Euler equations. In the periodic setting, we construct exact finite-time self-similar blowup solutions for $2/3<a<1$, with profiles that are neither focusing nor expanding. In the whole-space setting with a Neumann condition, we construct exact finite-time self-similar blowup solutions for the full range $0<a\leq1$, with profiles of focusing, non-expanding/non-focusing, or expanding form depending on the sign of the self-similar scaling parameter. The construction is based on a fixed-point formulation near the origin, followed by an ODE extension argument. We also establish regularity, asymptotic behavior, monotonicity properties of the profiles, and uniqueness up to the natural scaling invariance.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs exact finite-time self-similar blowup solutions for the Hou-Li model, a reduced model related to the 3D Euler equations, establishing existence, regularity, and uniqueness of blowup profiles across different parameter regimes.
AI.motivation: To study self-similar singularity formation in the Hou-Li model, which serves as a simplified analog of the axisymmetric Euler equations, to better understand potential finite-time blowup mechanisms in fluid dynamics.
AI.method: The construction uses a fixed-point formulation near the origin followed by an ODE extension argument to build exact self-similar blowup solutions.
AI.result: Constructs blowup solutions for 2/3 < a < 1 in periodic settings, and for 0 < a ≤ 1 in whole-space settings. Profiles can be focusing, non-expanding/non-focusing, or expanding depending on the scaling parameter sign. Regularity, asymptotic behavior, monotonicity, and uniqueness (up to scaling invariance) are established.
AI.conclusion: The work provides rigorous existence and characterization of self-similar blowup in the Hou-Li model, shedding light on singularity formation mechanisms relevant to the Euler equations.

title: Accelerated sampling using SamAdams variable timesteps and position-adaptive Langevin dynamics
summary: We introduce an accelerated Langevin-based sampling method that is based on two complementary devices: \emph{SamAdams} adaptive timestepping, which automatically shrinks the effective integration step in stiff regions of phase space using a relaxed stiffness monitor, and \emph{position-adaptive Langevin} (PAL) dynamics, which concentrates friction along the local force direction while preserving the canonical distribution as the exact invariant measure. The resulting combined scheme (SA-PAL) is implemented in a palindromic integrator which requires only one force evaluation per iteration through suitable organisation of the integration steps and by exploiting the rank-one-plus-scalar structure of the PAL friction tensor. We test the method on various model problems: the Rosenbrock function, a thin entropic channel, the Mueller-Brown potential, and a Bayesian parameterisation problem with a sparsity-inducing shrinkage prior. On the Rosenbrock and Mueller-Brown potentials mixing rates are improved by 1.5-3 times compared to fixed stepsize integration. Efficiency gains of more than an order of magnitude are documented in the other examples.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SA-PAL, a Langevin sampling method combining adaptive timestepping (SamAdams) and position-adaptive friction (PAL), achieving 1.5-3x faster mixing on simple potentials and >10x efficiency gains on complex problems.
AI.motivation: To accelerate Langevin-based sampling by addressing stiffness in phase space and anisotropy in friction, improving mixing rates and efficiency for challenging probability distributions.
AI.method: Combines SamAdams adaptive timestepping (shrinks steps in stiff regions via relaxed stiffness monitor) with position-adaptive Langevin (PAL) dynamics (concentrates friction along local force direction, preserving canonical distribution). Implemented in a palindromic integrator requiring only one force evaluation per iteration using rank-one-plus-scalar structure.
AI.result: 1.5-3x improved mixing rates on Rosenbrock and Mueller-Brown potentials; more than an order of magnitude efficiency gains on entropic channel and Bayesian sparsity-inducing shrinkage prior problems.
AI.conclusion: SA-PAL significantly outperforms fixed-step Langevin sampling, especially on problems with stiffness and anisotropy, while maintaining computational efficiency (single force evaluation per iteration).

title: Global well-posedness for general parabolic Anderson model on the whole plane
summary:
For every $ 0<κ<\sqrt 5-2$, we prove global existence for the two-dimensional generalized parabolic Anderson model on the whole space $\mathbb R^2$ with nonlinearity $F\in C_b^2(\mathbb R)$, driven by an enhanced noise $(η,Ψ)$. Here the noise $η$ has polynomially weighted spatial Besov--Hölder regularity $-1-κ$, and $Ψ$ is the corresponding renormalized second-order object. If $F''$ is globally Lipschitz, the solution is unique.
  The proof combines a weight-compatible annular high-low decomposition with a paracontrolled transport representation. The final remainder is estimated simultaneously in weighted $L^\infty$ norm and in a higher order weighted parabolic Hölder norm, with strictly different polynomial weights. This weight gap absorbs the polynomial losses arising from the enhanced noise and the transport coefficient. Several key refinements of the earlier works allow the maximum-principle and Schauder estimates to yield a global a priori bound for a larger range of $κ$. Uniqueness is proved in a time-dependent exponentially weighted topology.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global existence (and uniqueness under stronger conditions) for the 2D generalized parabolic Anderson model on ℝ² with a nonlinearity, driven by an enhanced noise of regularity -1-κ, for κ<√5-2, using a weight-compatible decomposition and paracontrolled transport representation.
AI.motivation: To extend the range of κ for which global solutions exist for the 2D generalized parabolic Anderson model, improving upon previous results.
AI.method: Combines a weight-compatible annular high-low decomposition with a paracontrolled transport representation. Estimates the remainder simultaneously in weighted L∞ and weighted parabolic Hölder norms with strictly different polynomial weights. The weight gap absorbs polynomial losses from the enhanced noise and transport coefficient.
AI.result: Global existence is proved for 0<κ<√5-2 (approximately κ<0.236) for the 2D generalized parabolic Anderson model. Uniqueness is proved under the additional condition that F'' is globally Lipschitz.
AI.conclusion: The refined approach using different polynomial weights for different norms, combined with maximum-principle and Schauder estimates, yields global a priori bounds for a larger range of κ than previously possible.

title: Incremental Tensor-Train Compression from Streaming TT-Formatted Data: Applications to Reduced-Order Modeling
summary: High-dimensional tensor data streams arise naturally in scientific and engineering applications, such as simulations of kinetic equations and quantum systems, where samples become available sequentially and are often already represented in compressed low-rank tensor formats. Existing streaming tensor-train (TT) algorithms typically construct or update representations from dense tensor data or randomized sketches. However, when high-dimensional data are generated directly in TT or related low-rank formats, reconstructing dense tensors solely for the purpose of compression is unnecessary and computationally prohibitive. We develop a deterministic incremental TT compression algorithm that operates directly on streaming TT-formatted data. Given a new TT tensor, the proposed method updates an accumulated TT representation through core-wise projection, residual orthogonalization, and adaptive enrichment, retaining only the complementary information that cannot be represented within a prescribed tolerance. By operating entirely at the level of TT cores, the algorithm avoids reconstructing either the incoming tensor or the accumulated full tensor. We establish approximation error bounds for the proposed incremental approach. Moreover, we show that the accumulated TT representation corresponds to a compressed analogue of standard proper orthogonal decomposition for full-order snapshot data, enabling reduced-order models to be constructed directly from streaming low-rank solution data through operations on TT cores, without first reconstructing full snapshots. Numerical experiments on parametric radiative transfer equations demonstrate that the proposed method achieves comparable reconstruction accuracy with substantially reduced wall time and yields efficient and accurate ROMs directly from compressed low-rank data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a deterministic incremental tensor-train (TT) compression algorithm that operates directly on streaming TT-formatted low-rank data, avoiding dense tensor reconstruction. It updates accumulated TT representations via core-wise projection, residual orthogonalization, and adaptive enrichment, with proven error bounds. The method enables reduced-order models (ROMs) directly from compressed data, demonstrated on parametric radiative transfer equations with improved efficiency and accuracy.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional tensor data streams (e.g., from kinetic equations, quantum simulations) often arrive sequentially in compressed low-rank TT formats. Existing streaming TT algorithms require dense tensor reconstruction or randomized sketches, which is computationally prohibitive when data is already in compressed form. The authors seek a method that operates entirely at the TT core level.
AI.method: A deterministic incremental TT compression algorithm with three key steps per incoming TT tensor: (1) core-wise projection onto the existing accumulated TT basis, (2) residual orthogonalization to extract new information, and (3) adaptive enrichment to expand the representation within a prescribed tolerance. All operations are performed directly on TT cores without reconstructing full tensors.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves approximation error bounds theoretically. Numerical experiments on parametric radiative transfer equations show comparable reconstruction accuracy to existing methods but with substantially reduced wall-clock time. The method also enables efficient and accurate ROMs directly from compressed low-rank data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed incremental TT compression algorithm is an efficient, deterministic alternative for streaming high-dimensional tensor data already represented in low-rank formats. It avoids the computational bottleneck of dense reconstruction and naturally connects to proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) for ROM construction.

title: Mixed Third-Order Flux Laws for Dual Cascade in the Stochastic SQG Equation
summary: We study dual-cascade flux laws for the stochastic forced--dissipative surface quasi-geostrophic (SQG) equation on a large periodic box. For statistically stationary solutions, under a weak anomalous dissipation assumption, we derive rigorous mixed third-order structure-function laws for the dual cascade: a Yaglom-type law for the direct cascade of surface potential energy (SPE) and an antisymmetrized mixed flux law for the inverse cascade of the Hamiltonian. In particular, the inverse Hamiltonian law appears to be new even as an explicit third-order structure-function relation. We also prove Onsager-type obstruction results showing that sufficiently regular stationary families cannot sustain the corresponding non-zero fluxes: $B^s_{3,\infty}$-regularity above the Onsager threshold $1/3$ rules out the direct SPE flux, while sufficient low-frequency Besov regularity rules out the inverse Hamiltonian flux. These results provide a rigorous formulation of the SQG dual-cascade phenomenology in a stochastic stationary setting.

AI.tldr: This paper rigorously derives dual-cascade flux laws for the stochastic forced-dissipative SQG equation, establishing Yaglom-type laws for direct SPE cascade and a new antisymmetrized law for inverse Hamiltonian cascade, along with Onsager-type regularity obstructions to non-zero fluxes.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous mathematical formulation of the dual-cascade phenomenology (direct SPE cascade and inverse Hamiltonian cascade) for the SQG equation in a stochastic stationary setting, which had previously lacked rigorous third-order structure-function laws.
AI.method: The authors study stochastic forced-dissipative SQG on a large periodic box with a weak anomalous dissipation assumption, deriving mixed third-order structure-function laws analytically and proving Onsager-type obstruction results using Besov space regularity thresholds.
AI.result: 1) Yaglom-type law for direct SPE cascade; 2) A new antisymmetrized mixed flux law for inverse Hamiltonian cascade; 3) Regularity thresholds: B^s_{3,∞} with s>1/3 rules out direct SPE flux, and sufficient low-frequency Besov regularity rules out inverse Hamiltonian flux.
AI.conclusion: The results establish rigorous foundations for SQG dual-cascade phenomenology, with the inverse Hamiltonian law being a novel explicit third-order structure-function relation, and the Onsager-type obstructions confirming that sufficiently regular solutions cannot sustain turbulent fluxes.

title: Solving Shifted Systems for Quasiseparable Matrices
summary:
In this paper we develop fast numerical algorithms for solving shifted linear systems with semidefinite quasiseparable matrices.
  A combination of Givens and hyperbolic plane rotations is used to update the Cholesky-type factorization of the input quasiseparable matrix by
  determining a factorization of its shifted version of the form $LDL^T$, where $L$ is lower triangular and $D$ is a signature matrix.
  If the shifted matrix is also definite then the Cholesky factorization of the shifted matrix is computed in a stable way by using orthogonal transformations. Since quasiseparability is maintained under diagonal shifting, a fast variant of the updating procedure using
  computations with generators is also devised. Numerical experiments show the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed algorithm.

AI.tldr: This paper develops fast numerical algorithms for solving shifted linear systems with semidefinite quasiseparable matrices, using Givens and hyperbolic rotations to update Cholesky-type factorizations while maintaining quasiseparable structure.
AI.motivation: To efficiently solve shifted linear systems with semidefinite quasiseparable matrices, which are common in various applications requiring fast and stable numerical methods.
AI.method: Combines Givens and hyperbolic plane rotations to update Cholesky-type factorization (LDL^T form) of quasiseparable matrices under diagonal shifting, including a fast generator-based variant leveraging maintained quasiseparability.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate effectiveness and robustness of the proposed algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithms provide fast, stable solutions for shifted linear systems with semidefinite quasiseparable matrices, with orthogonal transformations ensuring stability when the shifted matrix is definite.

title: Quantitative uniform resolvent estimates
summary: We derive quantitative uniform resolvent estimates for Schrödinger operators on the half-line with inverse-square potentials, which provide a sharp behaviour in the limit of large coupling. Our approach is based on a matrix representation of the boundary value of a weighted resolvent. The partial wave decomposition then turns these one-dimensional channel estimates into explicit weighted resolvent estimates for the Laplacian, its inverse-square potential perturbations and for the magnetic Laplacian with an Aharonov--Bohm potential. We also obtain exact Simon-type identities for the imaginary parts of the weighted resolvents of these operators.

AI.tldr: This paper derives sharp uniform resolvent estimates for Schrödinger operators with inverse-square potentials on the half-line, extends them to higher dimensions via partial wave decomposition, and obtains Simon-type identities for weighted resolvents.
AI.motivation: To obtain quantitative and sharp uniform resolvent estimates for Schrödinger operators with inverse-square potentials, particularly in the large coupling limit, and to extend these results to related operators like the Laplacian and magnetic Laplacian with Aharonov-Bohm potential.
AI.method: The approach uses a matrix representation of the boundary value of a weighted resolvent on the half-line, followed by partial wave decomposition to extend one-dimensional estimates to higher dimensions.
AI.result: Sharp uniform resolvent estimates for Schrödinger operators on the half-line with inverse-square potentials, explicit weighted resolvent estimates for the Laplacian and its perturbations, and exact Simon-type identities for the imaginary parts of weighted resolvents.
AI.conclusion: The derived estimates provide sharp behavior in the large coupling limit, and the method yields explicit results for a class of important operators including those with Aharonov-Bohm potentials.

title: An Arbitrary-Lagrangian-Eulerian solver for relativistic detonation waves
summary: In this paper we study the dynamics of relativistic detonation waves theoretically and numerically. The reaction is physically accounted for by an extra term in the definition of the total energy density and by an additional equation for the evolution of the mass fraction of the reactant, while leaving formally unmodified the equations of mass and energy-momentum conservation. In this way, the Rankine-Hugoniot relations maintain the same formal structure of the inert version. For the numerical solution we use a second order finite volume ALE scheme with TVD reconstruction, where the mesh velocity is chosen equal to the shock speed. We also adopt a locally implicit algorithm for the treatment of potentially stiff reaction source terms that arise in the equation of the reactant. We furthermore propose a particularly efficient algorithm for the conversion from the conserved to the primitive variables, which for the relativistic Euler equations is known to be nontrivial. Following this approach, we can successfully solve the Zel'dovich-von Neumann-Doering profile of a relativistsic detonation wave, up to Lorentz factors of the shock front $γ_S\sim 7$. Our analysis allowed us to highlight a new special relativistic effect, which has remained unnoticed so far. While in Newtonian detonations the Zel'dovich pressure jump decreases monotonically with the mass flux through the shock front, in the relativistic case it shows a minimum and then rises monotonically as a function of the mass flux. This may have interesting physical implications on the amount of energy that can be extracted from a relativistic detonation wave.

AI.tldr: This paper studies relativistic detonation waves, develops numerical methods (2nd order ALE scheme with TVD reconstruction, locally implicit algorithm for stiff reaction sources, efficient primitive variable conversion), solves the ZND profile up to Lorentz factor ~7, and discovers a new relativistic effect: the Zel'dovich pressure jump shows a minimum and then rises with mass flux, unlike the monotonic decrease in Newtonian detonations.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics of relativistic detonation waves, which have been less studied than their Newtonian counterparts, and to uncover special relativistic effects that may impact energy extraction from such waves.
AI.method: A second-order finite volume ALE scheme with TVD reconstruction is used; mesh velocity equals shock speed; locally implicit algorithm handles stiff reaction source terms; an efficient algorithm converts conserved to primitive variables (a nontrivial task in relativistic Euler equations).
AI.result: Successfully solved ZND profile of relativistic detonation waves up to Lorentz factor γ_S ~ 7. Discovered a new relativistic effect: the Zel'dovich pressure jump decreases then rises monotonically with mass flux, unlike the Newtonian monotonic decrease.
AI.conclusion: A new special relativistic effect in detonation dynamics was identified, which may have interesting physical implications for energy extraction from relativistic detonation waves.

title: Quantum Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Solving Integro and Fractional PDEs
summary: Quantum neural networks have emerged as powerful models for approximating nonlinear functions. Yet their use in solving integro-differential equations (IDEs) and fractional integro-partial differential equations (FIPDEs), which involve inherently nonlocal operators, remains unexplored. This work introduces a quantum physics-informed neural network (QPINN) framework that combines a quantum neural network with the governing equations of general nonlinear IDEs and FIPDEs. The proposed quantum network uses an affine feature map and variational quantum circuits to produce trial solutions with explicit trigonometric structure. We prove a quantitative $L^{2}(μ)$ universal approximation theorem for this architecture, achieving a convergence rate of $\mathcal{O}(n^{-1/2})$. This extends classical Fourier approximation theory to quantum circuits for physics-informed learning. We propose two QPINN variants: the numerical-quadrature QPINN (N-QPINN), which handles nonlocal integrals and fractional operators via high-order numerical quadrature while computing local derivatives through automatic differentiation of quantum trial solutions; and the auxiliary-function QPINN (A-QPINN), which eliminates numerical quadrature by introducing auxiliary variables that reformulate each integro-differential equation as an equivalent coupled system of partial differential equations, enabling a multi-output quantum neural network to simultaneously represent the solution and its associated variables. A series of numerical experiments demonstrates that the proposed QPINN framework accurately captures the behavior of nonlinear IDEs and FIPDEs and outperforms classical physics-informed neural networks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a quantum physics-informed neural network (QPINN) framework that combines quantum neural networks with governing equations to solve integro-differential equations (IDEs) and fractional integro-partial differential equations (FIPDEs). Two variants are proposed: N-QPINN (using numerical quadrature) and A-QPINN (using auxiliary variables). The framework achieves an L² universal approximation rate of O(n^{-1/2}) and outperforms classical PINNs.
AI.motivation: While quantum neural networks have been used for nonlinear function approximation, their application to IDEs and FIPDEs—which involve nonlocal operators—has not been explored. This gap motivates the development of a QPINN framework tailored for these challenging equations.
AI.method: A quantum physics-informed neural network combining affine feature maps and variational quantum circuits. Two variants: (1) N-QPINN uses numerical quadrature for nonlocal integrals/fractional operators and automatic differentiation for local derivatives; (2) A-QPINN reformulates each IDE/FIPDE as a coupled PDE system using auxiliary variables, solved by a multi-output quantum neural network.
AI.result: Proved a quantitative L²(μ) universal approximation theorem with convergence rate O(n^{-1/2}) for the proposed quantum architecture. Numerical experiments show QPINN accurately captures nonlinear IDE/FIPDE behavior and outperforms classical physics-informed neural networks.
AI.conclusion: The QPINN framework successfully extends quantum neural networks to solving integro-differential and fractional integro-partial differential equations, providing theoretical convergence guarantees and practical advantages over classical PINNs.

title: Fast summation on rectangular cuboids with arbitrary periodicity in the DMK framework
summary: Dual-space multilevel kernel-splitting (DMK) is a fast summation framework that combines ideas from the fast multipole method, Ewald summation, and multilevel summation. Originally formulated for free-space problems, and later extended to fully periodic problems on a cube, it decomposes the kernel interaction into a smooth global contribution and a hierarchy of localized interactions evaluated on an octree. We extend DMK to problems on rectangular cuboids with periodic boundary conditions in one, two, or three coordinate directions. The periodization leverages the fact that interactions on all tree levels below the root are localized, allowing for their evaluation with minimal modification on a cubical tiling of the domain. The remaining smooth root-level far-field contribution is evaluated in Fourier space, with Fourier series in the periodic directions and Fourier integrals in the free directions. For reduced periodicity, truncated kernels are used to regularize singular and near-singular Fourier kernels, yielding rapidly convergent trapezoidal discretizations and a unified treatment of all periodicities. For large-aspect-ratio cuboids, the root-level sum can be accelerated using the fast Fourier transform. We validate the method for the electrostatic potential and Stokeslet, stresslet and rotlet potentials, for all periodicities and a wide range of aspect ratios. Numerical experiments show that the periodization adds only a small overhead to the original free-space DMK algorithm, also for high-aspect-ratio cuboids. The resulting method provides a framework for applying DMK to problems with mixed periodicity on rectangular cuboids, and extends naturally to other non-oscillatory kernels for which a kernel split is available.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the Dual-space Multilevel Kernel-splitting (DMK) method from free-space and cubic periodic domains to rectangular cuboids with mixed periodicity (1D, 2D, or 3D), using Fourier-based root-level evaluation and truncated kernels for regularization, with validation on electrostatic and Stokes potentials.
AI.motivation: To extend the DMK fast summation framework to rectangular cuboids with periodic boundary conditions in one, two, or three coordinate directions, enabling efficient treatment of mixed-periodicity problems beyond cubic domains.
AI.method: The method periodizes DMK by: (1) evaluating localized lower-tree-level interactions on a cubical tiling with minimal modification, (2) computing the smooth root-level far-field contribution in Fourier space—using Fourier series for periodic directions and Fourier integrals for free directions, (3) employing truncated kernels to regularize singular/near-singular Fourier kernels for reduced periodicity, and (4) accelerating root-level sums with FFT for large-aspect-ratio cuboids.
AI.result: Numerical validation for electrostatic potential, Stokeslet, stresslet, and rotlet potentials across all periodicities and a wide range of aspect ratios shows the periodization adds only small overhead to the original free-space DMK, even for high-aspect-ratio cuboids.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a unified framework for applying DMK to mixed-periodicity problems on rectangular cuboids and extends naturally to other non-oscillatory kernels with available kernel splits.

title: Local well-posedness for fractional regularizations of hyperbolic systems of equations
summary: We establish a local well-posedness theory for a class of hyperbolic quasilinear evolution systems with fractional dissipation and non-local commutators of the fractional Laplacian. The analysis is motivated by the compressible isentropic Navier-Stokes equations with fractional diffusion and the compressible Euler alignment system with singular communication weights. Our approach does not rely on the cancellation condition previously used to recover the coercivity of the highest-order non-local terms. Instead, we exploit the self-adjointness of the fractional Laplacian together with an elementary algebraic identity for commutators of Fourier multipliers. Combined with the non-homogeneous Littlewood-Paley theory, these ingredietns yield the coercive structure and remains applicable for systems with variable coefficients. We further show that the associated solution operator fails to satisfy Banach's contraction principle. To overcome this difficulty, we establish a Hölder continuity estimate of order $\frac{1}{2}$, combine it with the Aubin-Lions compactness theorem and a tail-control argument, that yields the relative compactness of the solution operator, allowing the application of Schauder's fixed point theorem. The resulting theory provides local existence for arbitrary fractional orders $α\in(0,1)$ and identifies the obstacles for the local uniqueness of solutions in the presence of fractional commutators.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a local well-posedness theory for hyperbolic quasilinear evolution systems with fractional dissipation and non-local commutators, using Schauder's fixed point theorem instead of Banach's contraction principle, applicable for all fractional orders α∈(0,1).
AI.motivation: Motivated by compressible isentropic Navier-Stokes equations with fractional diffusion and compressible Euler alignment systems with singular communication weights.
AI.method: Exploits self-adjointness of the fractional Laplacian and an algebraic identity for commutators of Fourier multipliers, combined with non-homogeneous Littlewood-Paley theory, Hölder continuity estimates of order 1/2, Aubin-Lions compactness theorem, and a tail-control argument to apply Schauder's fixed point theorem.
AI.result: Establishes local existence for arbitrary fractional orders α∈(0,1) and identifies obstacles for local uniqueness of solutions in the presence of fractional commutators.
AI.conclusion: The approach does not rely on cancellation conditions, works for variable coefficients, and overcomes the failure of Banach's contraction principle through compactness methods.

title: On Fourier Phase Retrieval from Differential Intensity Measurements with Applications to Wavefront Sensing
summary: In this paper, we consider Fourier phase retrieval from differential intensity measurements, i.e., the problem of determining the phase of a complex-valued function from a series of intensity measurements differing only by slight modulations in Fourier domain. These modulations may be induced by optical elements such as prisms or phase plates, or via spatial-light modulators. Generalizing the principles behind the transport of intensity equation, we show that given such differential intensity measurements, the phase can be determined as the solution of certain partial differential or integro-differential equations. This is then used to design efficient reconstruction algorithms for a number of Fourier-type wavefront sensors, such as the pyramid wavefront sensor, commonly used in adaptive optics. Numerical experiments illustrate the usefulness of our proposed approach.

AI.tldr: The paper solves Fourier phase retrieval from differential intensity measurements by deriving PDE/integro-differential equations for phase recovery, generalizing transport of intensity principles, and applies this to wavefront sensors like pyramid sensors in adaptive optics.
AI.motivation: To determine the phase of a complex-valued function from intensity measurements that differ by slight Fourier domain modulations, with applications in adaptive optics and wavefront sensing.
AI.method: Generalizes transport of intensity equation principles to show phase can be determined as the solution of partial differential or integro-differential equations from differential intensity measurements.
AI.result: Efficient reconstruction algorithms for Fourier-type wavefront sensors (e.g., pyramid wavefront sensor), validated through numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PDE-based approach enables efficient phase retrieval for adaptive optics systems using differential intensity measurements.

title: Painlevé \uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral34\relax} and collisionless shock in the defocusing NLS equation with step-like initial data in the transition regions
summary: We consider the Cauchy problem for the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger (NLS) equation with step-like initial data. Using the nonlinear steepest descent method, we derive the long-time asymptotic expansion of the solution to the Cauchy problem in three distinct transition regions. In the first two transition regions, the leading-order asymptotics are characterized by Painlevé \uppercase\expandafter{\romannumeral34\relax}-type formula, while in the third one is a collisionless shock region, the leading-order asymptotics is describedin terms of Riemann theta functions. Our analysis is based on the Riemann-Hilbert formulation associated with the Cauchy problem of the defocusing NLS equation.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the long-time asymptotic behavior of solutions to the defocusing NLS equation with step-like initial data, revealing three distinct transition regions with asymptotics governed by Painlevé XXXIV-type formulas and Riemann theta functions using the nonlinear steepest descent method.
AI.motivation: To understand the long-time asymptotic behavior of the defocusing NLS equation with step-like (discontinuous) initial data, which exhibits richer transitional phenomena than smooth initial data.
AI.method: Nonlinear steepest descent method applied to the Riemann-Hilbert formulation of the Cauchy problem.
AI.result: Identified three distinct transition regions: two regions where leading-order asymptotics are given by Painlevé XXXIV-type formulas, and a collisionless shock region where asymptotics are described by Riemann theta functions.
AI.conclusion: The Riemann-Hilbert approach combined with nonlinear steepest descent effectively captures the rich asymptotic structure of the defocusing NLS equation with step-like data, revealing Painlevé transcendents and theta-function descriptions in different transition zones.

title: De la Vallée Poussin type approximation for solving some Fredholm integral equations
summary: In the present paper, we introduce a numerical method for second-kind Fredholm integral equations (FIEs) based on de la Vallée Poussin-type (VP) polynomial approximations at Jacobi zeros. This class of approximations offers several advantages over classical Lagrange interpolation at the same nodes. In particular, it guarantees uniformly bounded Lebesgue constants in suitable weighted function spaces and provides near-best uniform approximation for functions in these spaces, while also significantly mitigating the Gibbs phenomenon. We show how these properties can be exploited in the numerical solution of FIEs. In particular, the proposed approach effectively handles functions with possible algebraic endpoint singularities and kernel functions featuring weak singularities or highly oscillatory behavior. Under suitable assumptions, we prove stability and convergence of the method in weighted uniform spaces. Furthermore, we develop an efficient implementation based on the solution of a well-conditioned linear system. Numerical results confirm the theoretical error estimates and show that the proposed method achieves higher local accuracy than the corresponding Lagrange-based projection method.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a numerical method for solving second-kind Fredholm integral equations using de la Vallée Poussin-type polynomial approximations at Jacobi zeros, offering improved stability, reduced Gibbs phenomenon, and effective handling of singularities and oscillatory behavior.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of classical Lagrange interpolation (unbounded Lebesgue constants, Gibbs phenomenon) when solving Fredholm integral equations, especially for functions with endpoint singularities or oscillatory kernels.
AI.method: Uses de la Vallée Poussin-type polynomial approximations at Jacobi zeros, which provide uniformly bounded Lebesgue constants and near-best uniform approximation, implemented via a well-conditioned linear system.
AI.result: The method achieves stability and convergence in weighted uniform spaces, demonstrates higher local accuracy than Lagrange-based projection methods, and numerical tests confirm theoretical error estimates.
AI.conclusion: The VP-based method is a robust and accurate alternative for solving FIEs with singularities or oscillatory kernels, outperforming classical Lagrange interpolation approaches.

title: Pogorelov type interior $C^2$ estimate for sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds
summary: In this paper, we mainly study the interior $C^{2}$ estimates for a class of sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds. For $0\leq l<k< n$, we establish interior $C^{2}$ estimates at the center of a geodesic ball. Let $Ω$ be a bounded domain (with smooth boundary) on Riemannian manifold. For $0\leq l<k\leq n$, we establish Pogorelov type estimates on $Ω$ with the vanishing Dirichlet boundary condition.

AI.tldr: This paper studies interior C² estimates for sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds, establishing estimates at geodesic ball centers and Pogorelov type estimates under Dirichlet boundary conditions.
AI.motivation: To understand the regularity of solutions to sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds, which is fundamental for the theory of fully nonlinear elliptic equations in geometric settings.
AI.method: The authors use analytic techniques to derive interior C² estimates at the center of a geodesic ball, and establish Pogorelov type estimates on bounded domains with smooth boundary under vanishing Dirichlet condition.
AI.result: For 0 ≤ l < k < n, interior C² estimates at the center of a geodesic ball are obtained. For 0 ≤ l < k ≤ n, Pogorelov type estimates on a bounded domain Ω with vanishing Dirichlet boundary condition are established.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides important regularity estimates for sum Hessian quotient equations on Riemannian manifolds, contributing to the geometric analysis of fully nonlinear elliptic equations.

title: Hierarchical Muon: Tiled Newton-Schulz Updates for Efficient Muon Optimization
summary: Muon-type optimizers construct update directions for dense neural-network weights by applying a finite Newton-Schulz map to momentum-gradient matrices. For an $H \times W$ matrix, with $r=\min\{H,W\}$ and $s=\max\{H,W\}$, $K$ steps of the full-matrix Newton-Schulz update require $O(r^2 s K)$ work and couple all rows and columns through repeated Gram matrix products. We introduce Hierarchical Muon (HiMuon), a tiled Newton-Schulz scheme for Muon-type optimization. HiMuon partitions each momentum-gradient matrix into $T \times T$ tiles, applies the same finite Newton-Schulz map independently to each tile, and reassembles the results. For finite $T$ below the matrix dimensions, HiMuon defines a local matrix-function map rather than a convergent approximation to the full-matrix update: spectral interactions are preserved within tiles and discarded across tile boundaries. For fixed finite $T$, the leading Newton-Schulz work decreases to $O(H W T K)$, and the computation decomposes into independent small dense matrix operations. This structure enables tile-size-dependent GPU kernels, cross-layer batching, memory-bounded chunking, and runtime tile-size schedules. Experiments on transformer training and controlled matrix-function diagnostics show that HiMuon improves optimizer-step efficiency while keeping training behavior close to full-matrix Muon in the tested regimes.

AI.tldr: HiMuon is a tiled Newton-Schulz method for Muon-type optimizers that partitions momentum-gradient matrices into independent tiles, reducing computational cost from O(r²sK) to O(HWTK) while preserving training behavior close to the full-matrix version.
AI.motivation: Full-matrix Newton-Schulz updates in Muon-type optimizers are computationally expensive (O(r²sK)) and couple all rows and columns through repeated Gram matrix products, limiting efficiency for large neural network weight matrices.
AI.method: HiMuon partitions momentum-gradient matrices into T×T tiles, applies the same finite Newton-Schulz map independently to each tile, and reassembles results. This creates a local matrix-function map that preserves spectral interactions within tiles but discards cross-tile interactions.
AI.result: Leading Newton-Schulz work reduces to O(HWTK). The method enables tile-size-dependent GPU kernels, cross-layer batching, memory-bounded chunking, and runtime tile-size schedules. Experiments on transformer training and matrix-function diagnostics show improved optimizer-step efficiency with behavior close to full-matrix Muon.
AI.conclusion: HiMuon offers a computationally efficient approximation to full-matrix Muon optimization that maintains similar training behavior while significantly reducing computational cost through tiling.

title: Local stability for a class of Saint-Venant type inequalities
summary: We establish a local stability result for a class of Saint-Venant type inequalities. Given the solution $u$ of the Dirichlet torsion problem in a domain $Ω$, we consider shape functionals $\mathcal{J}(Ω)$ involving the integral of $j(u)$, where $j$ is convex and satisfies suitable structural assumptions. By Talenti's comparison principle, balls maximize $\mathcal{J}$ among sets of prescribed measure. We prove that this extremal property is stable in the class of nearly spherical sets: the deficit from the optimal value controls the square of the $H^{1/2}$-norm of the boundary perturbation. The argument relies on shape derivative techniques, including the computation of the second variation and the introduction of an adjoint state. As applications, the result covers several relevant examples, including the torsional rigidity, $L^p$-norms of the torsion function for $p\ge 2$, and Moser-Trudinger functional in dimension two.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that for Saint-Venant type inequalities where balls maximize certain shape functionals (like torsional rigidity), this extremal property is stable: the deficit from the optimal value controls the square of the H^{1/2}-norm of the boundary perturbation for nearly spherical sets. The proof uses shape derivative techniques and an adjoint state.
AI.motivation: To establish local stability for a class of Saint-Venant type inequalities, showing that when a domain is nearly spherical, the deviation from optimality quantifiably controls how far the domain is from a ball.
AI.method: The authors use shape derivative techniques, compute the second variation of the shape functional, and introduce an adjoint state to prove the stability estimate. They rely on Talenti's comparison principle as the starting point.
AI.result: The deficit from the optimal value controls the square of the H^{1/2}-norm of the boundary perturbation for nearly spherical sets. The result covers torsional rigidity, L^p-norms for p≥2, and Moser-Trudinger functional in 2D.
AI.conclusion: Saint-Venant type inequalities are locally stable: nearly optimal domains must be nearly spherical, with quantitative control.

title: Quasi-Feynman formulas that provide fast converging Chernoff approximations to solution of parabolic differential equation on the real line
summary: We construct explicit approximations to the solution of a second-order parabolic partial differential equation on the real line with variable coefficients. The method is based on Chernoff's product formula and uses a new operator-valued function defined through proper Riemann integrals over a bounded interval, which makes the approach readily usable in numerical practice. For sufficiently smooth initial data and coefficients, we prove that the resulting Chernoff approximations converge uniformly in space and time with a quadratic rate, improving the standard first-order estimate. The construction yields a new class of quasi-Feynman formulas that are neither grid-based nor Galerkin-type, but instead rely on semigroup theory and multiple bounded integrals. The theoretical findings are validated by symbolic computation, and the paper contributes both refined error bounds and a practical analytical tool for parabolic problems.

AI.tldr: The paper constructs explicit approximations for second-order parabolic PDEs using Chernoff's product formula with a new operator-valued function based on bounded Riemann integrals, achieving quadratic convergence rate—improving the standard first-order estimate—and validated by symbolic computation.
AI.motivation: To improve the convergence rate of Chernoff approximations for parabolic PDEs from first-order to quadratic, and to develop a practical, grid-free, non-Galerkin analytical tool based on semigroup theory.
AI.method: The method uses Chernoff's product formula with a new operator-valued function defined via proper Riemann integrals over bounded intervals, combined with semigroup theory and multiple bounded integrals to construct quasi-Feynman formulas.
AI.result: The Chernoff approximations converge uniformly in space and time with a quadratic rate for sufficiently smooth initial data and coefficients, improving the standard first-order estimate.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides both refined error bounds and a practical analytical tool for parabolic problems, validated by symbolic computation.

title: Instability of periodic waves in generalized Korteweg-de Vries-Burgers equation with monostable source
summary: We study periodic traveling waves in a general class of nonlinear dispersive equations with Burgers dissipation and a monostable Fisher-KPP reaction source. Under natural assumptions on the dispersion symbol, we prove the existence of small-amplitude periodic traveling waves bifurcating from a constant equilibrium by means of a Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction. We then investigate the spectral stability of these waves by combining Floquet-Bloch decomposition with perturbation theory for linear operators. It is shown that the Floquet spectrum of the linearized operator intersects the unstable complex half-plane, implying spectral instability with respect to localized perturbations. The results recover the recently established instability theory for the Korteweg-de Vries-Burgers-Fisher equation as a special case and provide a unified framework for a broad class of local and nonlocal dispersive systems. These results reveal a common instability mechanism generated by the interplay of dispersion, Burgers dissipation, and monostable reaction dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper studies periodic traveling waves in nonlinear dispersive equations with Burgers dissipation and Fisher-KPP reaction. Using Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction, the authors prove existence of small-amplitude periodic waves bifurcating from equilibrium, and then show their spectral instability via Floquet-Bloch analysis. The results unify and generalize known instability theory, including for the KdV-Burgers-Fisher equation, revealing a common instability mechanism from the interaction of dispersion, dissipation, and reaction.
AI.motivation: To understand the instability mechanisms of periodic traveling waves arising from the interplay of dispersion, Burgers dissipation, and monostable reaction, and to provide a unified theoretical framework that generalizes existing results for specific equations like KdV-Burgers-Fisher.
AI.method: Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction is used to prove existence of small-amplitude periodic traveling waves. Spectral stability is analyzed via Floquet-Bloch decomposition combined with perturbation theory for linear operators.
AI.result: The Floquet spectrum of the linearized operator intersects the unstable complex half-plane, implying spectral instability with respect to localized perturbations. The results recover the instability theory for the KdV-Burgers-Fisher equation as a special case.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a common instability mechanism driven by the interaction of dispersion, Burgers dissipation, and monostable reaction dynamics, providing a unified framework for a broad class of local and nonlocal dispersive systems.

title: Adaptive Iterative Numerical Homogenization for Quasilinear Nonmonotone Elliptic PDE
summary: We propose and analyze an adaptive iterative numerical homogenization method to approximate the solution of a class of quasilinear nonmonotone elliptic problems that is of multiscale nature. The method is based on the technique of the Localized Orthogonal Decomposition (LOD) applied to the linear problems in each step of a Kačanov iteration. In this approach, the multiscale basis is recomputed adaptively in each iteration and a linear problem is solved with this updated multiscale space, where in both steps the nonlinearity is evaluated using the approximation of the previous iteration. As a key component of the proposed approach, we present a locally computable error indicator, which at each iteration identifies the basis functions requiring updates, while previously computed basis functions are retained whenever possible. We provide a priori error estimates and show convergence of the method, requiring only higher integrability of the right-hand side, but no higher differentiability of the solution itself. Furthermore, we discuss how to adapt the proposed adaptive iterative LOD in the context of Newton's method as iteration scheme. Numerical experiments illustrate the theory and validate the applicability of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an adaptive iterative numerical homogenization method for quasilinear nonmonotone multiscale elliptic problems, using Localized Orthogonal Decomposition with adaptive basis updates and an error indicator, proving convergence with minimal regularity assumptions.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient numerical method for solving quasilinear nonmonotone elliptic problems with multiscale features, where the solution lacks higher differentiability.
AI.method: The method combines Kačanov iteration with Localized Orthogonal Decomposition (LOD), adaptively recomputing multiscale basis functions each iteration using a locally computable error indicator; also extends to Newton's method.
AI.result: Provides a priori error estimates and proves convergence of the method under only higher integrability of the right-hand side. Numerical experiments validate the theory.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive iterative LOD method effectively solves quasilinear nonmonotone multiscale elliptic problems with minimal regularity requirements, and is adaptable to Newton's method.

title: A Quasi-Variational--Hemivariational Inequality for the Convective Brinkman--Forchheimer Extended Darcy Equations with Bingham Fluids
summary: This paper is devoted to the analysis of a quasi-variational--hemivariational inequality associated with the convective Brinkman--Forchheimer extended Darcy (CBFeD) equations for Bingham fluids in both two and three spatial dimensions. The considered model describes incompressible fluid flow through porous media while incorporating convection, nonlinear damping effects, and Forchheimer-type resistance, together with non-smooth and non-convex slip boundary conditions. We first derive an appropriate weak formulation of the problem, which leads naturally to a Bingham-type quasi-variational--hemivariational inequality with a velocity-dependent constraint set. By employing the Kakutani--Ky Fan fixed point theorem, we establish the existence of weak solutions for the resulting multivalued quasi-variational inequality formulation of the CBFeD system. Furthermore, we prove that every weak solution of the associated quasi-variational inequality is also a solution of the corresponding quasi-variational--hemivariational inequality. The analysis presented in this work provides a rigorous mathematical framework for CBFeD models with Bingham fluids under non-monotone and non-smooth boundary interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes a quasi-variational–hemivariational inequality for the convective Brinkman–Forchheimer extended Darcy (CBFeD) equations governing Bingham fluid flow in porous media, establishing existence of weak solutions via the Kakutani–Ky Fan fixed point theorem.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous mathematical framework for CBFeD models with Bingham fluids under non-smooth and non-convex slip boundary conditions, which involve complex velocity-dependent constraints.
AI.method: The authors derive a weak formulation leading to a Bingham-type quasi-variational–hemivariational inequality, then apply the Kakutani–Ky Fan fixed point theorem to prove existence of weak solutions for the multivalued quasi-variational inequality formulation.
AI.result: Existence of weak solutions for the multivalued quasi-variational inequality formulation of the CBFeD system is established, and every such solution is also shown to satisfy the corresponding quasi-variational–hemivariational inequality.
AI.conclusion: The analysis provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for CBFeD models with Bingham fluids under non-monotone and non-smooth boundary interactions, applicable in both 2D and 3D.

title: A modified Euler-Maruyama method to simulate a one-dimensional sticky diffusion
summary: A sticky diffusion is a process that can stick to and detach from a lower-dimensional boundary. A challenge in simulating such a process is in capturing the change in dimension in a dynamically consistent way. We introduce a numerical algorithm to simulate a one-dimensional sticky diffusion, which sticks to and detaches from a point. Our method is a simple modification of the standard Euler-Maruyama scheme, which chooses with some probability between a reflected Euler-Maruyama update and a jump to the sticky point. We show how to choose this probability to be consistent with the generator of the desired dynamics, and we prove that our scheme converges weakly to a sticky diffusion with order 1.

AI.tldr: A simple numerical algorithm (modified Euler-Maruyama) simulates one-dimensional sticky diffusions that stick to and detach from a point, with weak convergence order 1.
AI.motivation: Sticky diffusions are challenging to simulate because the process changes dimensionality (from continuous diffusion to a fixed point), and existing methods lack dynamic consistency.
AI.method: A modified Euler-Maruyama scheme that probabilistically chooses between a reflected Euler-Maruyama update and a jump to the sticky point. The probability is chosen to match the generator of the sticky diffusion process.
AI.result: The scheme converges weakly to a sticky diffusion with order 1.
AI.conclusion: A simple, provably convergent numerical method for simulating one-dimensional sticky diffusions is presented, enabling reliable simulation of this dimension-changing process.

title: Gelfand-Shilov spaces and operators with ultradifferential weighted symbols on non-compact manifolds
summary: We invariantly define Gelfand-Shilov spaces on classes of non-compact manifolds with a certain ``structure at infinity''. We also construct and study a global calculus of pseudodifferential operators on such manifolds, locally defined by symbols satisfying estimates associated with weight sequences. The operators so obtained act naturally on the previously defined Gelfand-Shilov spaces. These generalise analogous functional spaces and operators defined on Euclidean spaces.

AI.tldr: This paper defines Gelfand-Shilov spaces on non-compact manifolds with structure at infinity and constructs a global pseudodifferential operator calculus on them, generalizing Euclidean counterparts.
AI.motivation: To extend Gelfand-Shilov spaces and pseudodifferential operator calculus from Euclidean spaces to non-compact manifolds with appropriate structure at infinity.
AI.method: Invariant definition of Gelfand-Shilov spaces on non-compact manifolds, and construction of a global pseudodifferential calculus using symbols with weight sequence estimates.
AI.result: Gelfand-Shilov spaces are invariantly defined, and a global pseudodifferential operator calculus is constructed that acts naturally on these spaces.
AI.conclusion: The work generalizes functional spaces and operators from Euclidean settings to more general non-compact manifolds.

title: Automated Galerkin time stepping in Irksome
summary: As the study of temporal and spatial discretization schemes continues to advance, recent work has focused on the use of Galerkin-in-time discretization schemes that enable broader structure-preservation than is known for Runge-Kutta integrators. While the promise of such discretizations is immense, their realization has, until now, generally relied on bespoke implementations that have limited their wider use. In this work, we present automation in Irksome for both discontinuous Galerkin and continuous Petrov-Galerkin time stepping of semidiscrete variational problems. The implementation supports auxiliary variables, flexible temporal quadrature, and monolithic algebraic solvers, and it enables switching between Runge-Kutta and Galerkin-in-time formulations with minimal changes to user code. Numerical examples illustrate accuracy, solver performance, and structure preservation across representative PDE systems.

AI.tldr: Irksome automates Galerkin-in-time discretizations (dG and cPG) for semidiscrete variational problems, enabling easy switching between Runge-Kutta and Galerkin time-stepping with support for auxiliary variables, flexible quadrature, and monolithic solvers.
AI.motivation: Galerkin-in-time discretizations offer better structure-preservation than Runge-Kutta methods, but have been limited by bespoke, non-generalizable implementations.
AI.method: Automated implementation in Irksome supporting both discontinuous Galerkin (dG) and continuous Petrov-Galerkin (cPG) time stepping for semidiscrete variational problems, with auxiliary variables, flexible temporal quadrature, and monolithic algebraic solvers.
AI.result: Numerical examples demonstrate accuracy, solver performance, and structure preservation across representative PDE systems.
AI.conclusion: The automation makes Galerkin-in-time methods more accessible and practical, allowing users to switch between RK and Galerkin formulations with minimal code changes.

title: A $Tb$ type theorem for suppressed kernels
summary: In this article, a non-homogeneous $Tb$ type theorem for arbitrary dimensional Calderón-Zygmund singular integral operators is proved. This is an extension of an analogous non-homogeneous $Tb$ theorem for the Cauchy transform, in the planar setting, due to Nazarov, Treil and Volberg. The novelties of the present work are the change of dimension and the fact that the operators to which the theorem applies are not necessarily antisymmetric. The techniques used in the proof include, among others, suppressed kernels, decompositions in $L^2(μ)$, where $μ$ is a Radon measure in $\mathbb{R}^d$, and a probabilistic argument resulting from taking averages of the operators involved.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a non-homogeneous Tb theorem for Calderón-Zygmund singular integral operators in arbitrary dimensions, extending a previous result for the Cauchy transform in the plane.
AI.motivation: To extend the non-homogeneous Tb theorem (previously proven only for the Cauchy transform in the plane) to higher dimensions and to operators that are not necessarily antisymmetric.
AI.method: The proof uses suppressed kernels, decompositions in L²(μ) (where μ is a Radon measure in ℝᵈ), and a probabilistic argument from averaging the operators involved.
AI.result: A non-homogeneous Tb type theorem for arbitrary dimensional Calderón-Zygmund singular integral operators is established.
AI.conclusion: The result generalizes the planar Tb theorem to higher dimensions and removes the antisymmetry requirement, broadening the class of operators covered.

title: \chisao{}: A GPU-Native Parallel Optimizer for Multimodal Black-Box Functions via Convergence-Anticonvergence Oscillation
summary: Finding all modes of a multimodal black-box function is a fundamental challenge in optimization, Bayesian inference, and scientific computing. Existing approaches -- basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent -- operate sequentially and cannot exploit the massive parallelism of modern GPU hardware. We introduce \chisao{} (\textbf{C}onvergence-\textbf{H}alt-\textbf{I}nvert-\textbf{S}tick-\textbf{A}nd-\textbf{O}scillate), a GPU-native population optimizer that runs an entire sample batch simultaneously and exploits a deliberate convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle to escape local traps while freezing confirmed modes. The structural move is asymmetric: samples that reach true peaks are frozen (``stuck'') and preserved, while the rest keep exploring via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients. Adaptive reseeding via two complementary strategies (Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster) maintains population diversity throughout. On all 42 functions of the Simon Fraser University optimization benchmark suite across dimensions $d \in \{2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64\}$, \chisao{} achieves \textbf{100\%} mode recovery where all CPU baselines collapse at $d \geq 8$ on the hardest multimodal functions, at up to \textbf{$34\times$} speedup over basin-hopping on functions where all methods succeed (Michalewicz $d=64$) and up to \textbf{$39\times$} on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid $d=64$, pure GPU dividend). All benchmarks evaluate the objective by value alone -- gradients come from finite differences -- so the reported speedups are a derivative-free worst case. Under substantial likelihood noise ($σ_{\mathrm{noise}}$ up to 1.0), mode detection remains 100\% reliable. The algorithm is available as a standalone open-source Python package on PyPI.

AI.tldr: CHISAO is a GPU-native population optimizer that finds all modes of multimodal black-box functions via a convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle, achieving 100% mode recovery on all 42 SFU benchmark functions up to dimension 64, with up to 39× speedup over CPU baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal optimization methods (basin-hopping, CMA-ES, multistart gradient descent) operate sequentially and cannot leverage modern GPU parallelism, limiting their scalability on high-dimensional problems.
AI.method: CHISAO runs an entire sample batch simultaneously on GPU using a convergence-anticonvergence oscillation cycle: frozen samples preserve confirmed modes while the rest explore via momentum-based anti-convergence and stochastically smoothed gradients, with adaptive reseeding (Repulse Monkey and Golden Rooster strategies) to maintain diversity.
AI.result: 100% mode recovery on all 42 SFU benchmark functions across d∈{2,4,8,16,32,64}, up to 34× speedup over basin-hopping on Michalewicz d=64, up to 39× on unimodal functions (Rotated Hyper-Ellipsoid d=64), and 100% reliable mode detection under substantial likelihood noise (σ_noise up to 1.0).
AI.conclusion: CHISAO demonstrates that a carefully designed GPU-native population algorithm with deliberate oscillation dynamics can drastically outperform sequential CPU methods for multimodal optimization, with open-source availability.

title: Evolving edge weights via local entropy flow and cohesion flow on graphs
summary: In this paper, we first propose two different quantities on graphs, namely local entropy and cohesion, then design two corresponding flows for edge weights: the local entropy flow and the cohesion flow. We establish the global existence and uniqueness of solutions for both flows and investigate their asymptotic behaviors, including the case that the limit goes to positive infinity. Moreover, they can be applied to fundamental network analysis tasks, including community detection and node classification. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves performance competitive with Ollivier Ricci flow and Lin-Lu-Yau Ricci flow on benchmark network analysis tasks. In experimental scenarios, we first apply the cohesion flow to evolve the edge weights of the graph, and then apply the local entropy flow to further update the resulting weighted graph. Both flows are computationally efficient, leading to a significant reduction in overall computational cost and improved scalability.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two new graph quantities (local entropy and cohesion) and their corresponding flows for edge weights, proving existence, uniqueness, and asymptotic behaviors. The method is applied to community detection and node classification, achieving competitive performance with Ricci flows while being more computationally efficient.
AI.motivation: To develop computationally efficient alternatives to existing graph flows (like Ollivier Ricci flow and Lin-Lu-Yau Ricci flow) for network analysis tasks, addressing scalability issues in community detection and node classification.
AI.method: Defines local entropy and cohesion quantities on graphs, designs two corresponding edge weight flows (local entropy flow and cohesion flow), and applies them sequentially: first cohesion flow then local entropy flow for network analysis tasks.
AI.result: Proves global existence, uniqueness, and asymptotic behaviors for both flows. Achieves performance competitive with Ollivier Ricci flow and Lin-Lu-Yau Ricci flow on benchmark tasks, with improved computational efficiency and scalability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed local entropy and cohesion flows offer a computationally efficient alternative for graph-based network analysis, maintaining competitive performance while reducing overall cost.

title: Zero-Shot Size Transfer for Neural ODEs on Sparse Random Graphs: Graphon Limits and Adjoint Convergence
summary: Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) model continuous-time graph dynamics by parameterizing Neural ODE velocity fields with Graph Neural Networks. Their local, size-independent filters suggest a zero-shot size-transfer principle: train on a small graph and deploy on larger, similar graphs without retraining. We develop a quantitative theory for this principle on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons. We consider Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as the infinite-node limits of the forward and adjoint GNDE systems, and establish well-posedness. For an $n$-node random graph with sparsity parameter $α_n$, we prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions at rate $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$, up to logarithmic factors, with high probability. We also establish uniform-in-time convergence bounds for adjoint systems governing hidden-state and parameter gradients. We further study discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training. Under explicit Euler discretization with $M$ steps, we show that DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent, with hidden-state and local parameter-gradient discrepancies of orders $O(1/M)$ and $O(1/M^2)$, respectively, up to sparsity and logarithmic factors. Experiments on HSBM and tent graphons support the theoretical rates, while zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes demonstrate accurate deployment of learned GNDEs on larger independently sampled graphs.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a rigorous theory for zero-shot size transfer in Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs), proving convergence rates of GNDEs to their infinite-node Graphon-NDE limits on sparse random graphs, establishing consistency of DTO and OTD training schemes, and validating the theory with experiments on synthetic graphons.
AI.motivation: GNDEs use local, size-independent filters, suggesting they can be trained on small graphs and deployed on larger ones without retraining (zero-shot size transfer). However, a rigorous theoretical foundation for this principle was lacking, especially on sparse random graphs.
AI.method: The authors model Graphon-NDEs and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as infinite-node limits of GNDEs. They prove trajectory-wise convergence at rate O((α_n n)^{-1/2}) for forward GNDEs and uniform-in-time bounds for adjoint systems. They also analyze DTO vs. OTD training under Euler discretization, showing asymptotic consistency with O(1/M) and O(1/M^2) discrepancies.
AI.result: Forward GNDEs converge to Graphon-NDEs at rate O((α_n n)^{-1/2}); DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent with O(1/M) and O(1/M^2) discrepancies; experiments on HSBM and tent graphons confirm theoretical rates; zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes validate the principle.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a rigorous theoretical basis for zero-shot size transfer in GNDEs, bridging discrete GNDEs to continuous graphon limits and providing convergence guarantees for both forward and adjoint dynamics under two training paradigms.

title: Preconditioning Magnetic Systems in Kohn-Sham Density Functional Theory
summary: The convergence of the self-consistent field iterations in Kohn-Sham density functional theory can be significantly hindered by the presence of small eigenvalues in the dielectric matrix, which are often associated with electronic phase transitions in magnetic systems. In this work, we study this type of convergence issues and propose a new preconditioning scheme to mitigate them. Our preconditioning scheme is inspired by the Stoner model and based on a non-interacting susceptibility that neglects orbital variations. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a range of ferromagnetic systems, showing that it can significantly reduce the number of iterations required to achieve convergence in the vicinity of magnetic phase transitions.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses convergence issues in Kohn-Sham density functional theory caused by small eigenvalues in the dielectric matrix during magnetic phase transitions, proposing a Stoner-model-inspired preconditioning scheme that reduces iteration counts in ferromagnetic systems.
AI.motivation: The self-consistent field iterations in Kohn-Sham DFT converge poorly when small eigenvalues appear in the dielectric matrix, especially near electronic phase transitions in magnetic systems. The authors aim to mitigate these convergence issues.
AI.method: The authors propose a preconditioning scheme inspired by the Stoner model, based on a non-interacting susceptibility that neglects orbital variations.
AI.result: The approach significantly reduces the number of iterations required for convergence in ferromagnetic systems near magnetic phase transitions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Stoner-model-based preconditioning effectively addresses convergence slowdowns caused by magnetic phase transitions in DFT calculations.

title: Algorithmic Foundations of Deep Learning: Complexity-Theoretic Rates and a Characterization of Universal Approximation
summary:
Feedforward neural network (NN) expressivity is typically studied by emulating optimal basis-expansion schemes. While powerful, this perspective is incomplete: it primarily captures complexity through regularity, and therefore does not distinguish intuitively simple and complicated objects with comparable regularity, such as the square-root function and a typical Brownian path.
  The guiding message is that neural networks should be viewed not only as flexible basis functions, but also as models of computation. If a function is computable by a real-valued circuit over a prescribed elementary gate language, then it can be computed to comparable accuracy by an NN with explicit depth, width, and non-zero-parameter bounds controlled by the depth, width, gate count, and gate structure. Thus, neural-network complexity is not governed by regularity alone, but also by algorithmic complexity. We then show that any definable NN model satisfying a natural parallelization condition, allowing possibly multivariate non-linearities such as attention or layer normalization, is a universal approximator if and only if it contains a non-affine nonlinearity.
  The scope of our theory is illustrated by deducing universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions, minimax-optimal approximation guarantees for Besov classes, logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions, and by showing that NNs can emulate numerical algorithms such as Newton-Raphson root finding and power iteration without architecture-specific arguments. Its precision is illustrated by shortest-path computation on $k$-vertex graphs: compiling the tropical dynamic-programming circuit yields NNs with O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially improving in 1/ε over the generic $O(ε^{-c k^2})$ Lipschitz-approximation scale, for a constant c>0.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that neural network expressivity should be understood not only through regularity (basis-expansion schemes) but also through algorithmic complexity (computable circuits). It shows that NNs can emulate real-valued circuits, yielding complexity bounds tied to circuit structure rather than just smoothness. The framework yields universal approximation guarantees, minimax-optimal Besov approximation, and logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions, and demonstrates that NNs can emulate algorithms like Newton-Raphson and power iteration. A key result: shortest-path computation on k-vertex graphs achieves O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially improving over the generic Lipschitz scale.
AI.motivation: Standard NN expressivity theory, based on emulating basis expansions, captures complexity mainly through regularity (smoothness).
