This fails to distinguish between functions with similar regularity but different intuitive complexity (e.g., square-root vs. Brownian path). The paper aims to provide a more complete perspective by viewing NNs as models of computation.
AI.method: The paper develops a framework where NNs are treated as computational models that can simulate real-valued circuits over a prescribed gate language. It establishes that any function computable by such a circuit can be approximated by an NN with depth, width, and parameter count controlled by the circuit's structure. It further proves that any definable NN model satisfying a natural parallelization condition is a universal approximator iff it contains a non-affine nonlinearity.
AI.result: The framework yields: (1) universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions; (2) minimax-optimal approximation for Besov classes; (3) logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions; (4) NN emulation of numerical algorithms (Newton-Raphson, power iteration); (5) for shortest-path on k-vertex graphs, O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially better than the generic O(ε^{-c k^2}) Lipschitz scale.
AI.conclusion: Neural network complexity is governed not only by regularity but also by algorithmic (computational) complexity. This perspective unifies and improves upon existing approximation results and provides a principled way to design efficient NNs for structured problems.

title: Holistic Multivariance Decomposition: Adapting Mode Interrelations in Low-Rank Tensor Approximations
summary: Low-rank tensor approximation is a foundational tool for multidimensional data analysis in scientific computing, classically dominated by Tucker and Canonical Polyadic (CP) decompositions. While widely adopted, these standard approximation schemes represent data as sums of rank-1 tensors formed via mode-wise outer products. This inherent mathematical structure captures the independent variations of individual modes but systematically neglects the mutual interactions and coupled dimensional interdependencies natively embedded within the tensor. To overcome this structural limitation, we introduce the Holistic Multivariance Decomposition (HMD) framework. HMD provides a novel tensor decomposition algorithm that explicitly models both isolated mode effects and higher order mutual relationships through specialized projection operators. Numerical evaluations focusing on three distinct benchmarks from various fields demonstrate that the proposed HMD framework consistently yields significantly lower reconstruction errors compared to both Tucker and CP decomposition. These results establish HMD as a robust, high fidelity computational method for resolving complex, deeply coupled multidimensional data structures in science and engineering applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Holistic Multivariance Decomposition (HMD), a new tensor decomposition framework that captures both isolated mode effects and mutual dimensional interdependencies, outperforming traditional Tucker and CP decompositions on benchmark tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard Tucker and CP decompositions model tensors as sums of rank-1 components via mode-wise outer products, which captures independent mode variations but systematically neglects mutual interactions and coupled dimensional interdependencies.
AI.method: HMD introduces a novel tensor decomposition algorithm that explicitly models both isolated mode effects and higher-order mutual relationships through specialized projection operators.
AI.result: Numerical evaluations on three distinct benchmarks across various fields show HMD consistently yields significantly lower reconstruction errors compared to both Tucker and CP decomposition.
AI.conclusion: HMD establishes itself as a robust, high-fidelity computational method for resolving complex, deeply coupled multidimensional data structures.

title: Numerical Approximation for Path-Dependent McKean-Vlasov Control with Non-Asymptotic Error Estimates
summary: Path-dependent McKean--Vlasov (MKV) control models large interacting populations with history-dependent dynamics and costs. This paper develops a unified approximation-and-learning framework for continuous time path-dependent MKV problem under open-loop controls. First, an Euler discretization scheme with piecewise-constant controls is shown to achieve a non-asymptotic error of $O(h^{1/4})$. Second, we establish a discrete dynamic programming principle and prove value equivalence between open-loop and history-dependent feedback controls, enabling optimization on a reduced filtration. Third, an interacting particle system is introduced to approximate the continuous-time value, yielding an overall error bound of $O(h^{1/4}) + O(M^{-γ})$ for $M$ particles and an explicitly given $γ> 0$. Finally, we propose a fully implementable neural-network policy-gradient method using pathwise features. Numerical experiments, including a path-dependent linear-quadratic benchmark, demonstrate the effectiveness of the algorithm.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a unified approximation-and-learning framework for continuous-time path-dependent McKean-Vlasov control problems, achieving error bounds of O(h^{1/4}) + O(M^{-γ}) and demonstrating effectiveness via a neural-network policy-gradient method with numerical validation.
AI.motivation: To provide a theoretically grounded and practically implementable framework for solving path-dependent McKean-Vlasov control problems, which model large interacting populations with history-dependent dynamics and costs.
AI.method: Euler discretization with piecewise-constant controls; discrete dynamic programming principle; interacting particle system approximation; neural-network policy-gradient method with pathwise features.
AI.result: Non-asymptotic error O(h^{1/4}) for discretization; overall error bound O(h^{1/4}) + O(M^{-γ}) for M particles; value equivalence between open-loop and history-dependent feedback controls.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is both theoretically sound and numerically effective, as demonstrated by a path-dependent linear-quadratic benchmark.

title: Valuing American options and Flexible Forwards contracts in time-dependent models
summary: A flexible forward (FF) is a customized FX hedging instrument that guarantees a fixed exchange rate while letting the holder choose the delivery date within a pre-agreed window. It is therefore an American-style option on timing, and its valuation must respect the volatility skew of the underlying currency pair. We price FF contracts (and, more generally, American options) under a time-inhomogeneous Heston model which captures the forward-skew term structure while preserving analytical tractability through a recursive (matrix) Riccati solution for the joint characteristic function. Extending the integral-equation (decomposition) approach to time-dependent coefficients, we derive a Volterra equation characterizing the early-exercise surface. The expectation in the decomposition formula is evaluated by two complementary spectral methods: a double cosine (COS) expansion of the transition density, and a damped-Sinc (DSINC) local-basis scheme that is more accurate and stays robust when a low Feller ratio or large vol-of-vol induces Gibbs oscillations in the COS series. Benchmarked against a penalty-iteration MCS-ADI finite-difference solver, both methods price a contract in about 1-2 seconds, roughly an order of magnitude faster than the finest finite-difference grid, while DSINC improves median accuracy over COS by about a factor of twelve. The experiments also show that the early-exercise surface is a substantially nonlinear function of the variance, contrary to the linear-in-variance approximation common in earlier work.

AI.tldr: This paper prices Flexible Forward FX contracts (American-style options on timing) under a time-inhomogeneous Heston model using recursive Riccati solutions and two spectral methods (COS and DSINC), achieving 1-2 second pricing with DSINC showing 12x better accuracy than COS, and revealing that the early-exercise surface is nonlinear in variance.
AI.motivation: To accurately price Flexible Forward contracts—customized FX hedging instruments with embedded American timing options—while respecting the volatility skew of the underlying currency pair, which prior linear-in-variance approximations fail to capture.
AI.method: Extends the integral-equation decomposition approach to time-dependent coefficients under a time-inhomogeneous Heston model; solves the joint characteristic function via recursive matrix Riccati equations; evaluates the expectation using two spectral methods: double COS expansion and damped-Sinc (DSINC) local-basis scheme.
AI.result: Both spectral methods price a contract in ~1-2 seconds (an order of magnitude faster than the finest finite-difference grid); DSINC improves median accuracy over COS by a factor of ~12 and stays robust under low Feller ratio/high vol-of-vol where COS suffers Gibbs oscillations.
AI.conclusion: The early-exercise surface is substantially nonlinear in variance, contradicting earlier linear approximations; DSINC is the recommended method for practical pricing of Flexible Forward contracts.

title: Error-Conditioned Neural Solvers
summary: Neural surrogate models offer fast approximate mappings from PDE parameters to solutions, but they typically treat solving as a purely statistical task: once trained, they struggle to correct their own constraint violations and extrapolate beyond the training distribution. Recent hybrid methods promote physical correctness by targeting the PDE residual via gradient descent or Gauss--Newton steps, but inherit the compute cost and instability of the underlying classical optimizers. We show, theoretically and empirically, that numerically minimizing the PDE residual can be an unreliable proxy for reconstruction accuracy in ill-conditioned systems, explaining why these methods often do not make accurate predictions despite achieving low residuals. We propose error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS), built on a different principle: rather than an optimization target, the PDE residual field is passed as a direct input to the network at each iteration, enabling it to read the spatial structure of its own errors and learn an update policy to iteratively correct its predictions. Across four PDE families, ENS attains the highest prediction accuracy in the large majority of settings, with gains reaching $10\times$ on turbulent Kolmogorov flow, while avoiding the expensive compute cost of hybrid methods. ENS's learned correction policy generalizes under distribution shift, including zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer, where its relative advantage is largest in the ill-conditioned regimes where residual minimization is least reliable. Project website: https://neuralsolver.github.io/.

AI.tldr: ENS is a neural PDE solver that uses the PDE residual as input rather than an optimization target, achieving 10x better accuracy on turbulent flows while avoiding expensive compute costs and generalizing across distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Existing neural surrogate models struggle to correct constraint violations and extrapolate; hybrid methods that minimize PDE residuals via gradient descent are computationally expensive and unreliable for ill-conditioned systems.
AI.method: Error-conditioned Neural Solver (ENS) passes the PDE residual field as a direct input to the network at each iteration, allowing it to learn an update policy that iteratively corrects its predictions based on the spatial structure of its own errors.
AI.result: ENS achieves highest prediction accuracy across 4 PDE families, with 10x gains on turbulent Kolmogorov flow; generalizes under distribution shift including zero-shot parameter changes and cross-equation transfer; avoids expensive compute costs of hybrid methods.
AI.conclusion: Numerical residual minimization can be an unreliable proxy for accuracy in ill-conditioned systems; ENS's learned correction policy is most advantageous precisely where residual minimization fails.
